verbatim_text: So users can spend five minutes either trying to craft a prompt or trying to interact with a language model. And once the five minutes are up, we take the transcripts from each and have the language model predict how the user would rate various test cases. So we're going to use the accuracy of the language model on these predictions as the metric. OK, so we're measuring the accuracy of the final specification after five minutes. The dark gray bar is our static prompting baseline. The colored bars are different types of interactive prompting. We conduct these experiments across three domains. And here are our results. So we can see that in the content and email domain, the interactive approaches beat the static prompting baseline, but not in the moral domain. So we see that interactively eliciting preferences sometimes outperform static prompting. But what I'm going to show next is that these results can be improved by equipping language models with better user models. So how is this a user modeling problem? So recall from the beginning of the talk that a user-agent dialogue can be thought of as a POMDP over beliefs about the user. Each time we get a new user answer, we update our beliefs about the user, which informs what the next question we want to ask is.
